text:
Deep learning-based generative models have shown tremendous promise in time series data. Although deep neural networks can be very efficient and show great performance in the time series data analysis, the models’ performance is often restricted by the amount, quality, and availability of the training data. As collecting new data can be costly, as class imbalance is present, or as data privacy laws are stringent in many domains, Data Augmentation (DA) has become a very popular approach to cope with the shortages of training data. Starting from trivial transformations, such as adding random noise or randomly shuffling particular parts of the time series, up to generating brand new time series for the training set with the aid of generative models, all these variations belong to the more general class of DA.

This paper conducts a systematic review of the state of the art of time series data augmentation (TSDA) and introduces a taxonomy. Many techniques have been proposed in the literature, ranging from simple transformations to deep learning-based models such as Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) and Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs). However, existing studies are limited to a simple survey of methods and do not examine in depth the efficiency of the different instantiations of the models, nor do they investigate the data quality metrics employed to evaluate the quality of generated data, nor do they discuss the difficulties associated with each model. Therefore, this paper aims to systematically summarize the progress of TSDA based on the proposed taxonomy and compare the performances of the various instances of the models proposed in the literature while analyzing the data augmentation quality evaluation metrics and difficulties of each model. This paper is expected to help the researchers and engineers working on the TSDA to better understand the existing models and choose the suitable techniques.
